title: Object Appraisal: Part History and Intended Use
narrationScript: Picture two welds with two identical indications. Same size, same amplitude, same depth. One is accepted and left in service. The other is cut out that afternoon. And both decisions are right. How can that be? Because evaluation does not end at the screen. The same reflector means different things in different objects, and the discipline of weighing the object itself, how it was made, what it has lived through, and what it must do next, is what evaluators call object appraisal. Start with manufacturing history. Every product form has a signature set of discontinuities, and knowing the part's biography tells you what an indication plausibly is. Castings carry shrinkage, gas porosity, and sand inclusions. Forgings carry laps, bursts, and stringers strung out along the flow lines. Rolled plate carries laminations lying flat at mid thickness. Welds carry the whole catalog, lack of fusion at the bevels, slag between passes, porosity, and cracks. Heat treatment can add quench cracks of its own. And repairs deserve special suspicion. A repair weld concentrates everything that can go wrong, restraint, hydrogen, awkward access, all in one spot, so a records check that turns up a prior repair at your indication's location should raise your eyebrows. Manufacturing records also tell you whether the indication is old news. If the baseline inspection at fabrication recorded the same reflector, same size, same place, you are looking at a stable original condition. If the baseline was clean, something has changed. And change is the most important finding in all of in service inspection. That brings us to service history. Ask what the part has lived through. Cyclic loading grows fatigue cracks from stress risers, and it grows them from exactly the kinds of features that were acceptable on day one. Corrosive service thins walls and pits surfaces. Wet hydrogen sulfide service can blister and crack steel from the inside out. Long years at high temperature let material creep. A single overload event, a hard lift, a pressure excursion, can leave cracks behind. The same three millimeter reflector reads very differently in a part with forty years of pressure cycles than in one fresh from stress relief. The gold standard question is always growth. An indication that has not moved across three inspection intervals is a known, stable condition. An indication that was smaller last year is an active mechanism, and amplitude tables were never written to bless a growing crack. Then weigh intended use. Discontinuity significance is a product of the flaw and the stress field it lives in. A planar flaw standing perpendicular to the principal tensile stress is the worst case. The same flaw lying parallel to the stress, or sitting at the neutral axis of a bending member, or living in a compression zone, may be nearly harmless. Cyclic tension is far more dangerous than static load, because fatigue feeds on every cycle. Service temperature matters, because steel loses toughness as it cools, and a flaw that is tolerable in a warm vessel can be critical in a cold one. Consequence belongs on the scale too. A flaw in a fracture critical crane hook and the same flaw in a handrail do not deserve the same anxiety, and the codes that govern them know it. Be clear about whose call this is. The construction code's acceptance criteria are workmanship rules, quality control lines drawn at what a competent shop can routinely achieve. They are not predictions of failure. When an owner wants to accept something beyond the workmanship line because the stress analysis supports it, that is a fitness for service evaluation, fracture mechanics applied to real loads, and it belongs to engineering under whatever authority the governing code and the owner's program grant. Your job as a Level Two is to feed that decision flawless data and to document the appraisal trail, the indication's size, location, and orientation, the history facts you relied on, the use assumptions, and the basis for the disposition, with names, levels, and dates. The test of the record is simple. Another qualified inspector, reading it cold, should arrive at the same conclusion. That is what makes an appraisal defensible instead of an opinion.
imagePrompt: An inspector spreads decades-old fabrication drawings and inspection reports across a table beside a corroded pressure vessel nozzle, flaw detector and angle-beam probe resting on the weld seam under portable work lights.

sections.heading: Same Echo, Different Verdicts
sections.body:
Two identical indications — same equivalent reflector size, same amplitude versus reference, same depth — can rightly receive opposite dispositions in two different parts. That is not inconsistency; it is **object appraisal**: the recognition that an indication's *significance* lives in the object, not on the screen.

The amplitude comparison from the previous lessons gets an indication onto the table — recorded, located, sized against a reference. Appraisal then asks three questions the screen cannot answer: **How was this part made?** (What can this indication plausibly be?) **What has it survived?** (Is it original, or did service create it?) **What must it do next?** (What stress, environment, temperature, and consequence ride on it?)

A Level II is not asked to be a fracture mechanic. You *are* asked to gather and weigh these facts, recognize when they change the picture, apply the governing criteria honestly, and document the reasoning so the decision can be defended years later — in an audit or, worse, a failure investigation.

sections.heading: History of the Part: How It Was Made
sections.body:
Product form is destiny. Each manufacturing route leaves a signature family of discontinuities, and the part's biography tells you what your indication plausibly is:

- **Castings**: shrinkage (piping, sponge), gas porosity, sand and slag inclusions, hot tears at section changes.
- **Forgings**: laps and seams near the surface, internal bursts, hydrogen flakes, inclusion **stringers elongated along the flow lines** — orientation you can predict from the forging direction.
- **Rolled plate**: laminations lying flat at mid-thickness — flat, stable, and parallel to the surface.
- **Welds**: lack of fusion at bevel faces, slag between passes, porosity, root flaws, and cracks — each tied to process and position.
- **Heat treatment**: quench cracks at section changes and holes.

Two records matter as much as the form. **Repairs**: a documented repair weld at the indication's location concentrates restraint, hydrogen, and awkward access — treat it as a prime suspect. **Baseline NDT**: if fabrication records show the same reflector, same size and place, you have a stable original condition; if the baseline was clean, something changed in service — a different conversation entirely.

sections.caption: The object-appraisal flow: confirmed indication data weighed against part history and intended use to select the acceptance basis and disposition.

sections.heading: History of the Part: What It Has Survived
sections.body:
Service history converts an indication from a geometry problem into a life-cycle question. The exposures that matter:

- **Cyclic loading** grows fatigue cracks from stress risers — weld toes, ratholes, nozzle corners — starting from features that were perfectly acceptable on day one.
- **Corrosion** thins walls, pits surfaces, and turns smooth geometry into stress risers.
- **Hydrogen and wet H₂S service** can blister steel and crack it from the inside (hydrogen-induced and stress-oriented hydrogen-induced cracking).
- **High-temperature service** accumulates creep damage at welds and headers.
- **Overload events** — a hard lift, a pressure excursion, a derailment — leave cracks where the analysis said none could be.

The sharpest tool in in-service evaluation is **comparison against prior results**. An indication unchanged across several inspection intervals is a known, stable condition that can often live under the original acceptance basis. An indication that was absent — or smaller — last time is an **active mechanism**. No workmanship amplitude table was ever written to bless a growing crack; growth escalates the finding regardless of amplitude.

sections.title: An indication that grows is a different animal
sections.body: Stable since fabrication = a manufacturing condition, judged against the original criteria. **New or grown since the last examination = an active mechanism** — fatigue, corrosion, creep, hydrogen — and the evaluation escalates immediately, whatever the amplitude. This is why baselines and repeatable setups (same block, same reference, same datum) are worth their paperwork.

sections.heading: Intended Use: Stress, Orientation, Temperature, Consequence
sections.body:
Discontinuity significance is the product of the flaw **and** the stress field it lives in:

- **Orientation**: a planar flaw perpendicular to the principal tensile stress is the worst case; the same flaw parallel to the stress is far less severe. A mid-thickness lamination in plate is usually benign under in-plane load — and becomes serious the moment through-thickness tension appears (as at a welded attachment).
- **Position in the stress field**: surface flaws see the highest bending stresses and feed fatigue; a flaw at the neutral axis of a bending member loafs in low stress; compression zones are forgiving.
- **Static vs cyclic**: fatigue feeds on cycles; a flaw tolerable under static load can walk through the section under cyclic tension.
- **Temperature**: steel toughness falls as temperature drops — the identical flaw is more dangerous in cold service.
- **Consequence**: fracture-critical components (a crane hook, a lifting lug, a reactor nozzle) rightly carry tighter criteria than a handrail. Codes encode this as joint classes, service classes, and examination categories.

Intended use is why the question "is 3 mm acceptable?" has no universal answer — only an answer *in this part, in this service*.

sections.caption: The same planar flaw in two objects: across cyclic tension at the surface fiber it is critical; parallel to static stress at the neutral axis it is nearly inert.

sections.heading: Workmanship vs Fitness-for-Service — and Writing It Down
sections.body:
Construction-code acceptance criteria are **workmanship rules**: quality-control lines set at what a competent shop routinely achieves. Exceeding them does *not* mean the part will fail — and meeting them does not license abuse. When an owner wishes to retain a flaw beyond the workmanship line because analysis supports it, that is a **fitness-for-service (FFS) evaluation**: fracture mechanics applied to the actual flaw, loads, and material toughness, performed by **engineering** under the authority the governing code and owner's program define. A Level II's role there is to deliver flawless flaw data — size, location, orientation, growth history — not to grant the acceptance.

Every appraisal ends as a record. A defensible one contains: the indication data (location from datum, depth, length, orientation, amplitude vs reference); the **history facts relied on** (product form, repair records, baseline results, service exposure); the **use assumptions** (loading, temperature, environment, consequence class); the **basis applied** (code clause or referenced engineering evaluation); and the names, certification levels, and dates of everyone who decided anything. The test: another qualified inspector, reading it cold, reaches the same conclusion.

interactive.title: One Indication, Four Objects
interactive.intro: A 4 mm-high planar indication at 60% of reference amplitude sits at mid-wall on the fusion line of a 25 mm butt weld. You will meet it four times — and the right answer changes with the object.

steps.prompt: First meeting: the weld is brand new, in a pressure vessel still under construction. How is the indication dispositioned?
options: Accept it — 60% of reference is comfortably low
Apply the construction code's acceptance criteria for planar indications exactly as written — workmanship rules govern new construction
Commission a fracture-mechanics evaluation before anything else
Reject it — planar indications are forbidden by every code at any amplitude
steps.feedback: New construction is judged against the construction code's workmanship criteria — indication type, amplitude, and length limits as written. FFS has no role yet, and neither blanket acceptance nor blanket rejection is a code basis.

steps.prompt: Second meeting: the same indication turns up during in-service inspection of a cyclically loaded crane-runway girder weld — and the fabrication baseline shows nothing at this location. What changes?
options: Nothing — 60% of reference is still 60% of reference
It now represents probable initiation and growth in service: treat it as an active fatigue flaw and escalate, regardless of its modest amplitude
It can be downgraded because it is subsurface
Only the original construction code's amplitude table matters, forever
steps.feedback: A clean baseline means the reflector appeared in service. In a cyclic-tension member that is presumptive fatigue growth — an active mechanism that workmanship amplitude tables were never written to bless.

steps.prompt: The owner's engineer says: 'Go ahead and accept it under fitness-for-service — the stresses there are low.' As the Level II, your correct move is:
options: Accept it — an engineer said so verbally
Reject the weld to stay safe and close the file

Document the indication completely and refer the FFS disposition to engineering under the owner's written program — characterizing flaws is your job; FFS acceptance is an engineering disposition with its own authority and paperwork
Re-scan at higher gain until the indication exceeds reference, forcing a rejection
steps.feedback: FFS is an engineering function performed under a defined written authority with documented analysis. Your contribution is complete, accurate flaw data — and a record showing who dispositioned what, on what basis. Verbal acceptance is not a basis.

steps.prompt: Which record makes this appraisal defensible five years from now?
options: "Indication acceptable per discussion."

Full flaw data (location from datum, depth, length, orientation, amplitude vs reference), the history facts used (baseline result, service exposure, repair records), the use assumptions, the acceptance basis cited, and names, levels, and dates
A photograph of the A-scan with no commentary
The instrument's data-logger file alone
steps.feedback: A defensible appraisal lets another qualified inspector reach the same conclusion cold: complete flaw data, the facts relied on, the basis applied, and accountability. Everything else is an opinion with a date on it.

checkQuestions.question: A near-surface indication in a forged shaft is elongated parallel to the forging flow lines. Part history makes the most plausible identification:
options: A shrinkage cavity from solidification
A lap or inclusion stringer aligned with the metal flow
A lamination from plate rolling
Weld spatter
checkQuestions.explanation: Forgings carry laps, seams, and inclusion stringers strung out along the flow lines; shrinkage belongs to castings and laminations to rolled plate. Manufacturing history predicts what an indication can plausibly be.

checkQuestions.question: An indication is found during in-service inspection. The most important single question for its significance is:
options: Whether its amplitude exceeds 50% of reference
Whether it was present, at the same size, in the baseline and prior examinations — or is new or grown
Whether it is on the access side of the weld
Whether the part was stress relieved
checkQuestions.explanation: Stable-since-fabrication indications are original manufacturing conditions; a new or growing indication is an active mechanism (fatigue, corrosion, creep, hydrogen) and escalates the evaluation regardless of amplitude.

checkQuestions.question: Which combination makes a given planar flaw MOST significant?
options: Parallel to static compressive stress at the neutral axis
Perpendicular to cyclic principal tensile stress at the surface fiber
Parallel to in-plane load at mid-thickness
Any orientation in a low-consequence, lightly loaded part
checkQuestions.explanation: Fatigue feeds on cyclic tension, surface fibers carry the highest bending stress, and a planar flaw normal to the principal tensile stress presents its full face to the load — the worst case on every axis of the appraisal.

checkQuestions.question: Construction-code acceptance criteria are best described as:
options: Predictions of the flaw size that will cause failure
Workmanship quality-control limits reflecting what competent fabrication routinely achieves — not failure predictions
Suggestions that inspectors may waive for schedule
Fracture-mechanics calculations performed on each indication
checkQuestions.explanation: Workmanship criteria draw a quality line, deliberately conservative and easy to apply. Accepting flaws beyond that line requires a fitness-for-service evaluation by engineering under defined authority — not a field reinterpretation.

checkQuestions.question: Two identical flaws sit in identical welds, but one vessel operates at −40 °C and the other at +200 °C. The cold-service flaw is generally:
options: Less significant, because thermal stresses are lower
Equally significant — temperature does not enter appraisal
More significant, because steel toughness falls with temperature, reducing the tolerable flaw size
Impossible to inspect ultrasonically
checkQuestions.explanation: Fracture toughness of steels drops as temperature falls toward and below the transition range, so the same flaw is closer to critical in cold service — intended use, including service temperature, belongs in every appraisal.

references: CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §6.2.1 (history of part)
CP-105-2024 UT Evaluation Course §6.2.2 (intended use of part)
Fitness-for-service practice per standards such as API 579-1/ASME FFS-1 — cited generically
In-service inspection comparison practice (baseline and successive examinations) — general industry practice
